paragraphs.body: Today I will be talking about the dialogue and character elements of writing fiction. To start with dialogue, in the class I’ve realized the importance of dialogue but also the importance of how it is formatted. It helps you to understand better about the characters while also being able to enhance the plot and create tension.

annotations.comment: Good focus. Your opening sentence is a little elementary.

paragraphs.body: Like Anna Lamott mentioned in Bird by Bird, “Good dialogue is such a pleasure to come across while reading, a complete change of pace from description and exposition and all that writing. Suddenly people are talking, and we find ourselves clipping along” (p.14). I feel like it adds more character to ones story and if its well written it can make you feel like you’re a part of the conversation or even make it feel like you’re sitting there listening to her tell it. Which can be a tricky thing to do, let alone perfect.

annotations.comment: I love that quote, great choice. Thanks for citing!

paragraphs.body: I am going to compare two short stories in which I feel one shows good dialogue and one I feel not so much. This is all opinion based on how I felt while reading these pieces. To start with the good, I chose the story Sibling Rivalry by Michael Byers. Throughout the story, Byers writes to reveal the characters without overexplaining them. Since it is a short story, throwing in long descriptions would make it un-enjoyable to read, this is unlike how Byers wrote it. He made it apart of the dialogue, without rambling descriptions. Byers also has a good flow of humor and tension with out making it look out of place. What I felt made Byer’s dialogue better than most was his use of punctuation and italicization. From Siblings Rivalry Michael Byers writes, “Matt-is-bothering me! And I told him, nicely, to stop jumping out and scaring me, and then he keeps doing it!.... My parents still don’t have cookies.” The italicization helps to really emphasis the tone of Julies words or just words in general when italicization is used in this way. I also think the spacing with the dialogue in this story made it a good example and if there was no spacing he added the character’s name next to the dialogue, so you didn’t get confused. I chose this story because it made the quote I chose from Lamott feel true.

annotations.comment: Always hear for a story’s dragging.
annotations.context_left: d one I feel not so

annotations.text: This is all opinion based on how I felt while reading these pieces
annotations.comment: No need to say this — the whole paper is your opinion.

paragraphs.body: A story I felt didn’t show strong dialogue and could use some improvement was The Perfect Girlfriend. I understand I am not a professional writer, just this dialogue was personally hard for me to read. Majority of the dialogue being built into the rest of the story makes it harder to read and feels like the flow wasn’t steady. From The Perfect Girlfriend, “David looked down at his phone to see a notification from “Ivy” that read “Happy 1 Year, I love you.” He replied, “Happy Anniversary Baby, I love you too” and raced out the door, leaving work at 5:00 pm on the dot.” Personally, for me reading this dialogue felt very rushed and doesn’t fit the flow of good dialogue.

annotations.text: Majority of the dialogue being built into the rest of the story makes it harder to read and feels like the flow wasn’t steady.

annotations.comment: Yes, I agree that the formatting doesn’t help the flow of the dialogue here.
annotations.context_left: A story I felt didn’t show strong
annotations.context_right: and could use some improvement was The Perfect Girlfriend. I understand I am not a professional wri

paragraphs.body: Moving on to the character element of writing I think it is equally as important as dialogue and that they go hand and hand. Like Anna Lamott said in Bird by Bird, “Knowledge of your characters also emerges the way a Polaroid develops: it takes time for you to know them” (p.45). Character development helps to draw the reader into a story and make them want to continue reading.

Biasedly I think my story The Early Train, shows character development between Lily and Weston. Lily’s personality starts to show throughout the story with her inner thoughts coming too. While Weston’s character can be seen coming forward in the notes he wrote to Lily. From The Early Train, “I hadn’t talked to anyone new since freshman year, and now I am a junior. Plus, my New Year’s resolution was to put myself out there more. This was my chance. What could go wrong?” but later in the story this conversation, “I took a deep breath. This was my chance. “Ralph polo ralphs club.” He looked at me, his eyebrows raising in surprise. “Yeah… how’d you know?” I laughed, the tension easing a little. “I have a weird habit of smelling all the men’s colognes at ULTA. Yours is one of my favorites.” I braced myself, worried I sounded weird.” I feel like these two lines of dialogue really show the growth in characterization with Lily. Weston’s character development isn’t as strong as Lilys character development starting with, “ ‘Mind if I sit here?’ ” and ending with, “ ‘Lily, I haven't smiled like that in a while. I’d like to get to know you better. Text me if you feel the same way. ;) – Weston’ ”. Even though wasn’t as impactful as Lilys character I still think its impactful to the story.

annotations.text: While Weston’s character can be seen coming forward in the notes he wrote to Lily.
annotations.comment: Should be part of the prior sentence.
I’d like to see a little more analysis here than you’ve presented, but I agree overall.
annotations.context_right: The Early Train, shows character development between Lily and Weston. Lily’s personality starts to

paragraphs.body: I don’t think I’ve figured everything out yet when it comes to writing fiction, but I do know I’ve improved. I’m noticing the little things now, like how one line of dialogue can say more about a character than a full paragraph. I’ve started trusting that characters can reveal themselves slowly, that their voices will show through, and that sometimes the most meaningful parts of a story are the quiet ones. This class helped me understand that writing doesn’t have to be perfect it just has to feel real.

annotations.comment: Sophie, there are quite a few grammatical errors here, which make comprehension slower — in the future, the Writing Center can help you with those concerns. But I did feel like you wrote this in your own voice, and while I felt the analysis was too brief to be fully effective, I appreciate that you found much in line with the essay’s prompt. Have a lovely summer!
